sections.heading: Problem Statement
sections.content:
The cortex indexer was created to allow developers and AI agents working on Python projects to build a hierarchical, queryable scaffold around a codebase. It indexes every function, module, and documentation section — along with their relationships, change history, and documentation staleness — into a local SQLite database.

This gives all project participants a reliable way to understand project structure and dependencies without reading every source file. It is especially valuable for AI agents: when an agent runs out of context, a new agent can use the index to pick up where the previous one left off, query what has been verified, and understand what still needs attention. The staleness checker ensures that documentation stays honest as code evolves, and the verification + gate system ensures that nothing merges with unresolved documentation drift.

sections.heading: Personas
sections.content:
Two personas use the indexer:

**Developer** — builds and maintains the project, runs CLI commands directly, reviews impact reports, approves PRs. The developer is the human in the loop for the GitHub action gate.

**Agent** — a single agent type that does all automated work: reads the index for project context, makes code changes, updates documentation, runs tests, marks nodes clean with a reason, and generates the impact report. There is no separate review agent persona — one agent type handles everything.

sections.heading: User Stories
sections.content:
**Scanning:**

1. As a **developer**, I want to run `cortex init` on a new project so that the index is created and all existing nodes have a baseline established in one step.
2. As a **developer**, I want to run `cortex build` after pulling or making changes so that newly added functions and modules are indexed without disturbing existing staleness signals.
3. As a **developer**, I want `cortex init` to be safely re-runnable on an existing project so that I can reset the index to a clean baseline without needing a separate command to remember.
4. As an **agent**, I want to query all functions and modules in a project so that I can understand project structure without consuming context on raw file reads.
5. As an **agent**, I want to find all nodes that depend on or call a specific function so that I can assess the blast radius of a change before making it.
6. As an **agent**, I want to search the index by keyword so that I can locate the relevant function or module without knowing the exact file path.
7. As a **developer**, I want `cortex build` to skip re-scanning all file types (`.py`, `.json`, `.md`) whose mtime hasn't changed so that no-change builds complete in milliseconds even on large projects with many documentation files.

**Staleness:**

8. As a **developer**, I want to know which functions have code that changed without matching doc updates so that I can catch documentation drift before it merges.
9. As a **developer**, I want staleness detection to run in under one second on unchanged projects so that I can use it as a pre-commit check without friction.
10. As an **agent**, I want to check whether a node's documentation is stale before reading it so that I know whether to trust the existing description or go read the source directly.
11. As an **agent**, I want to see which doc sections are LINKED_STALE and which code change triggered it so that I know exactly what prose to update without re-reading every linked function.
12. As an **agent**, I want to mark a node clean with a reason and my identity so that subsequent agents and the impact report can see what was already verified and why.

**Verification & Gate:**

13. As a **developer**, I want a GitHub action that fails if there are any unverified stale nodes so that stale documentation cannot merge without being addressed.
14. As a **developer**, I want agent-verified nodes to be distinguished from human-verified nodes so that the gate can be tightened to require human sign-off in a future version.
15. As an **agent**, I want to generate a deterministic impact report summarizing all changes since the last merge — what changed, what was verified and by whom, what tests passed — so that the developer has a single artifact to review before approving the PR.

**Test Runner:**

16. As a **developer**, I want to run all tests that cover a specific function by naming that function so that I can validate a targeted change without running the full suite.
17. As a **developer**, I want to run all tests that cover any function in a module by naming that module so that I can validate a module-level refactor in one command.
18. As an **agent**, I want to run all tests associated with a function or module and have the results stored in the index so that test outcomes appear as verification evidence in the impact report.

**Renames & Refactoring:**

19. As an **agent**, I want the index to automatically detect when a function was moved unchanged to a different module so that its history and verification record carry over to the new node ID without manual intervention.
20. As a **developer**, I want NOT_FOUND on genuinely deleted or refactored functions — not just moved ones — so that the index correctly distinguishes a move from a rewrite or split.

sections.heading: Verification Model
sections.content:
The indexer uses the **Impact Report + PR Approval** model for V1.

**How it works:**

1. An agent makes code changes, updates documentation, runs tests, and marks stale nodes clean with a reason (recorded as `verified_by='agent'` in `node_verification`).
2. The agent runs `cortex report`, which generates a deterministic impact report by reading the index — changed nodes since the last checkpoint, verification status per node, test results, and any CONTENT_UPDATED doc sections. No LLM is involved; it is a pure SQL read and runs in milliseconds.
3. The impact report is attached to the PR. The developer reads it as part of code review and approves the PR. That approval is the human review of the batch.
4. The GitHub action checks two things: `cortex check` passes (no unverified stale nodes remain), and an impact report was generated for this change set.

**V1 gate behavior:** agent-verified is sufficient to pass the gate. The `verified_by` field already distinguishes `'agent'` from `'human'` in the `node_verification` table, but the gate does not require `'human'` in V1. This keeps the workflow unblocked while the agent system is being established.

**History's role:** the `node_history` table is the foundation of the impact report and the verification audit trail. It is not exposed as a browsing feature in V1 — it is infrastructure. The `cortex history` CLI command and checkpoint/pruning UI are deferred to V2.

**Rename detection:** when a function goes NOT_FOUND, the indexer checks whether any newly discovered node has the same `code_hash`. If yes, it is treated as a move: history and verification migrate to the new node ID and a row is added to `node_renames`. If no, it is a genuine deletion or refactor and stays NOT_FOUND. This correctly handles:

| Scenario | code_hash match | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Function moved to another module unchanged | Yes | Auto-renamed, history migrates |
| Function refactored in-place | No (same node ID) | DESC_UPDATED on existing node |
| Function split into two | No | Old node NOT_FOUND, new nodes are new |
| Function deleted | No | NOT_FOUND, no migration |

sections.heading: V1 Requirements
sections.content:
**Scanning:**
- [x] `cortex init` creates `.cortex/cortex.db`, runs a full scan, and establishes all `code_hash`/`desc_hash` baselines — the safe first-run command
- [x] `cortex build` (discovery-only, always safe) adds newly discovered nodes only; preserves staleness signals on existing nodes — no destructive variant
- [x] `cortex init` is re-runnable: if `.cortex/cortex.db` already exists, prompts for confirmation before wiping and rebuilding all baselines
- [ ] Scans .py files via AST: functions, methods, classes, modules
- [ ] JSON doc files scanned and indexed with section-level granularity
- [ ] dFlow decorators (`@workflow`, `@task`, `@test_workflow`) detected and stored as `dflow_meta`
- [ ] Edges auto-generated: `composes` (module->function), `delegates_to`, `validates`
- [ ] Hash-similarity rename detection: on NOT_FOUND, check if any new node has the same `code_hash`; if yes, auto-migrate history and add a `node_renames` row

**Staleness (two-column model: own_status + link_status):**
- [ ] Mtime-based fast path skips unchanged files
- [ ] Per-function AST hash comparison for changed files
- [ ] `DESC_UPDATED` / `NOT_FOUND` / `UNVERIFIED` / `VERIFIED` own_status values
- [ ] `LINKED_STALE` / `BROKEN_LINK` link_status values for doc sections whose linked code node changed — cannot be verified away, must be updated
- [ ] `cortex check` with `--fail-on` for CI gates (`none` / `stale` / `unverified` / `any`)
- [ ] `cortex mark-clean` records agent or human verification with a reason; `verified_by` distinguishes `'agent'` from `'human'`

**Verification & Gate:**
- [ ] `node_verification` table stores `verified_by`, `reason`, and `code_hash_at` per node
- [ ] `cortex report` generates a deterministic impact report: changed nodes since last checkpoint, verification status, test results, CONTENT_UPDATED sections — pure SQL read, no LLM
- [ ] GitHub action runs `cortex check --fail-on any` and verifies an impact report exists; V1 accepts agent-verified as passing

**History (infrastructure only — no CLI browsing in V1):**
- [ ] Every node upsert records a `node_history` row (`INITIAL`, `CONTENT_ONLY`, `DESC_ONLY`, `CONTENT_AND_DESC`)
- [ ] History rows are the source data for `cortex report` — no separate aggregation needed
- [ ] Auto-pruning: keep most recent 100 rows per node (prevents unbounded growth)

**Test Runner:**
- [ ] `cortex test` runs pytest with JUnit/JSON report parsing
- [ ] `--node <node_id>` scopes run to all tests with `validates` edges to that specific function
- [ ] `--path <module_id>` scopes run to all tests covering any function in that module
- [ ] Results stored in `test_runs` and `test_results` tables
- [ ] Test results surfaced in `cortex report` as verification evidence per node

sections.heading: Non-Goals (Scope Shield)
sections.content:
The indexer does NOT do any of the following in V1:

- **Not a linter or code formatter** — staleness detection only, no automatic fixes
- **No automatic doc generation** — the index flags stale nodes; agents or developers write the updates
- **No multi-language AST parsing** — Python only for per-function granularity; other file types fall back to whole-file hashing
- **No cloud or remote database** — local SQLite only
- **No incremental file watching** — re-run `cortex build` manually after changes
- **No `cortex history` CLI browsing** — history rows are collected and used by `cortex report`, but there is no interactive history viewer in V1
- **No configurable gate strictness** — V1 gate accepts agent-verified; `--strict-human` mode requiring human sign-off is V2
- **No VCS integration** — jj/git change IDs in history rows are V2; the current system is file-system-based
- **No manual rename declaration** — V1 uses hash-similarity auto-detection only; `cortex link --rename` is V2 for edge cases
- **No `cortex build --full`** — the full-reset path is `cortex init` (re-runnable); there is no destructive flag on `cortex build`

sections.heading: The Icebox
sections.content:
Features deferred from V1. History data is collected; browsing UX waits.

**Verification & History:**
- `cortex history` CLI command — browse per-node change history (data already written in V1)
- `cortex history checkpoint` — collapse old rows beyond the auto-prune limit
- `--strict-human` gate mode — require `verified_by='human'` for CI to pass, not just agent-verified
- VCS change IDs in history rows (jj/git commit descriptions as staleness context)

**Renames:**
- `cortex link --rename old_id new_id` — manual rename declaration for cases hash-similarity can't detect (e.g. function renamed AND refactored in the same change)

**Scanning:**
- JavaScript/TypeScript AST scanning for frontend code
- Import-graph edge detection (static analysis of `import` statements)
- File watcher mode (auto-rebuild on save)

**Infrastructure:**
- Remote SQLite or Postgres backend for team-shared indexes
- `cortex diff` — show what changed between two checkpoints
- Auto-generate doc stubs for undocumented nodes
